APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it tackles lexical entailment in context, a core lexical semantics problem, and introduces two datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN) and cross-lingual experiments (Sections 5 and 6). The methods and evaluation match community standards (use of GloVe/Context2Vec/BiVec, logistic regression baselines, weighted F1, comparisons to prior work and a new SOTA on Shwartz & Dagan (2015); Sections 6–7). The problem formulation and positioning cite and build on relevant literature (Turney & Mohammad, Erk & Pado, Melamud et al.), making the paper clearly targeted to the NLP/ML research audience (Sections 1, 3, related work references).

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—lexical entailment grounded in context—which is directly relevant to QA and NLI (Intro). It introduces two new evaluation resources (CONTEXT-PPDB and the large CONTEXT-WN) and shows that simple contextualized representations and similarity features yield significant gains and a new state-of-the-art on prior contextual relation tasks (Sections 5–7). The method generalizes cross-lingually (English–French results) and captures sense-sensitivity and entailment directionality (Section 8), making it likely to be adopted and extended; while impactful and broadly useful, the work is evolutionary rather than wholly transformative.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (clear Intro → Methods → Experiments → Analysis → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (contextualized word representations, new datasets, improved SOTA). Notation is generally consistent and defined (e.g. ~wl, ~wr, Cl/Cr, Context2Vec), though there are a few small inconsistencies/typos (e.g. repeated ~wl,c2v, the odd ‘F = ~60’ fragment). Figures/tables are referenced (Table 4/5, Figure 2) but not present here, and the provided text contains duplicated paragraphs and LaTeX/artifact noise that slightly reduces readability; these are presentation issues rather than problems with the structure or clarity of the core arguments.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper reframes lexical entailment as an in-context problem and introduces new evaluation resources (CONTEXT-WN and augmented CONTEXT-PPDB) and cross-lingual tests, which are novel contributions. However, the core modeling techniques largely compose existing ideas: context masking and aggregation (citing Erk & Pado; Thater et al.), Context2Vec for shared word/context embeddings (Melamud et al.), and similarity-based supervised classifiers (Baroni et al., Turney et al.). Thus the work is an applied, non-trivial combination and empirical validation for a new task setting rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic advance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Turney & Mohammad 2013; Roller et al. 2014; Shwartz & Dagan 2015; Context2Vec) and to cross-lingual embedding baselines (BiVec, BiCVM) and PPDB features (Sections 6–7). Comparisons appear fair: same data splits, weighted F1 metric, and the authors use the same logistic-regression setup as prior work (Section 6.1) and report concrete improvements (e.g. +4.8 F1 over Shwartz & Dagan, Section 7.1). The paper also includes ablations and analyses (Sections 7–8) that acknowledge when prior representations (e.g. Context2Vec) are competitive and analyze differences from earlier contextualization methods (Erk & Pado; Thater et al.), indicating comprehensive and fair comparison.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s central claim—that contextualized word representations and word-context similarity features improve lexical-entailment detection—is empirically supported by experiments on two new datasets and cross-lingual transfer (dataset construction in Section 5, experimental setup in Section 6, and reported gains in Section 7). The authors also probe context-sensitivity and masking choices (Section 8), and compare to strong baselines including PPDB features and prior work. However, some methodological details are under-specified (exact mask computation and full feature list) and no statistical significance tests are reported, and the automatically generated CONTEXT-WN could introduce dataset artifacts despite the authors’ controls; these are minor issues that do not overturn the experimental evidence.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents solid, multi-faceted experiments (Sections 5–7) including two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), cross-lingual transfer, and comparison to prior state-of-the-art, supporting its main claims. Appropriate ablations and sensitivity analyses are reported (Section 8: Macro-F1 sensitivity to context; Section 8.3 comparing masked vs mean context representations), and related work is comprehensively cited. Limitations are discussed to some extent (e.g. poor performance of raw Context2Vec contexts, and noting that only Logistic Regression was used with minimal tuning in Section 6.1), and reproducibility is reasonably addressed through dataset construction details and listed embeddings, though some implementation/hyperparameter specifics and final release of annotation details are deferred. Overall the evaluation is thorough with minor gaps (limited classifier variation and some reproducibility details).
